text:
ByTodd Harrison

AEI Foreign and Defense Policy Working Paper Series

July 31, 2024

While the Summer Olympic Games take place in Paris, another global competition is underway hundreds of miles overhead in space. US military leaders havewarnedthat China is “growing its military space and counterspace capabilities at breathtaking pace,”Russiamay be developing a nuclear anti-satellite weapon, and the US military is investing tens of billions of dollars in a new generation of satellites it calls theProliferated Warfighter Space Architecture. At the same time, a single US company, SpaceX, now controls nearly two-thirds of all operational satellites in space, and NASA is racing to return astronauts to the Moon before a joint Chinese-Russian effort gets there first.

These developments naturally draw comparisons to the space race that occurred during the Cold War, leading some analysts to conclude that we are now in aSpace Race 2.0. But the competition today is more complex and multidimensional. The Cold War space race was a competition between the two superpowers, and it had a clear starting point with the launch ofSputnik in 1957and a clear finish line on the Moon. The current competition is more like the Space Olympics because it involves dozens of nations competing across multiple events simultaneously.

The three main “events” to watch in the Space Olympics are the competitions in commercial space, military space, and lunar exploration. The stakes are highest in these events because nations are competing to build an enduring economic and military advantage—and shape the de facto rules for how the space economy will operate in the future. Below is your guide to each of these competitions explaining what is at stake, how the competition is shaping up, and which nations are favored to win.

Read the full working paperhere.
title: It’s Not a Space Race, It’s the Space Olympics | American Enterprise Institute - AEI
